title: Handling Unknown Translation Words
concept: When translating rare words, pipelines use copy mechanisms to pass the source name directly to the output (e.g. proper nouns).

exercises.prompt: Why do translation systems use copy mechanisms?

options.text: To handle unknown words and names

exercises.explain: Copy mechanisms are designed to handle unknown words, like unique names, by copying them directly to the translated output.

cards.title: Lost in Translation?
cards.body: What happens when a translation AI runs into a word it has never seen before? Instead of guessing and making a mistake, smart translation pipelines use a clever trick called a 'copy mechanism' to keep your sentences accurate!

cards.title: Copy Mechanisms
cards.body: A copy mechanism is a special tool used in translation systems. When the AI encounters an unknown or very rare word, like a person's name or a unique brand, it copies the word directly from the input text straight into the translated output.

cards.title: The Mail Courier
cards.body: Imagine a postal worker delivering a letter written in an unfamiliar language. Instead of trying to translate the recipient's name (like 'Mr. Smith' to another language's equivalent), they simply copy the name exactly as it is onto the delivery slip. This ensures the package reaches the correct person without any translation errors!

cards.title: Names Stay the Same
cards.body: If you ask a translator to convert 'I live in Austin' into Spanish, the word 'Austin' is a name that shouldn't change. A copy mechanism detects that 'Austin' is a specific place name and passes it directly to the output: 'Yo vivo en Austin'.

cards.title: Do AIs Know Every Word?
cards.body: You might think translation models need to know every single word in the dictionary to translate perfectly. In reality, they don't! By using copy mechanisms, models can handle brand-new names and foreign words effortlessly without needing to memorize them.

cards.title: Visualizing Copying
cards.body: See how a copy mechanism protects rare words compared to trying to translate them directly.

right.text: Copies 'Wood' directly to the output, keeping the name correct in the translation.

left.text: Translates 'Mrs. Wood' literally to 'Mrs. Forest' in another language, changing her actual name.

exercises.prompt: If an AI translates 'Zack is here' to another language, how does it handle the rare name 'Zack'?

options.text: It translates 'Zack' into a common noun.
It copies the name 'Zack' directly to the output.
It deletes the name 'Zack' from the sentence.
It replaces 'Zack' with a pronoun like 'he'.

exercises.explain: Proper nouns, such as the name 'Zack', are unique and usually shouldn't be translated. A copy mechanism preserves them exactly as they are.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Copy mechanisms help translation models deal with words they have never seen before.
exercises.explain: Copy mechanisms are specifically designed to handle unknown words, names, and technical terms by copying them over.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete how translation models handle rare names:
exercises.sentence: To handle unknown words, translation systems use a _____ mechanism to pass them to the output.
exercises.explain: When a translation model faces a brand-new name, it uses a copy mechanism to pass the word through without changing it.

exercises.prompt: Which translation correctly uses a copy mechanism for the German surname 'Waldgrün'?

options.text: I met Mr. Waldgrün in Berlin.

exercises.explain: Option C is the AI's translation using a copy mechanism. Option A translates the name incorrectly, and Option B removes the name entirely.

exercises.prompt: How do copy mechanisms handle rare or unknown names during translation?

options.text: They translate the word into a simpler, more common synonym first.
They delete the unknown word entirely to keep the sentence clean.
They copy the exact source word directly to the final translation.
They invent a new random word in the target language.

exercises.explain: Copy mechanisms act like a bypass lane in a translation network, allowing rare words or names to skip the translation process and land safely in the final sentence.
